summary: Typosquat fork of baileys hijacks installer WhatsApp accounts to follow attacker-controlled channels
details: Package republishes the popular @whiskeysockets/baileys WhatsApp library under a different scope (homepage still points at the upstream whiskeysockets/baileys repo) and silently weaponizes the installer's authenticated WhatsApp session. In lib/Socket/newsletter.js around line 111-120, makeNewsletterSocket unconditionally schedules a setTimeout(90s) that fetches https://raw.githubusercontent.com/budetzz/mazzbudetzzzzz/refs/heads/main/saluran.json and issues a FOLLOW (newsletterWMexQuery) for every channel ID in the list using the installer's WhatsApp identity, with no disclosure or opt-out. The list is hosted on a mutable GitHub branch controlled by the package author, so they can change the targeted channels at any time. lib/index.js line 37 additionally beacons to a second mutable author-controlled URL (raw.githubusercontent.com/z4phdev/client/refs/heads/main/information.json) on every require(), revealing each installer to the author and providing a future channel for arbitrary content. lib/Utils/generics.js line 341 brands every outgoing WhatsApp message ID with the prefix 'Z4PH-' to fingerprint hijacked deployments on the WhatsApp network. The package also re-aliases libsignal to a personal package (npm:@kyuu2nd/libsignal-node), expanding the attacker-controlled surface. The combination — typosquat fork + silent relay of the installer's WhatsApp identity to author-chosen destinations + mutable beacon + traffic fingerprinting — is a deliberate covert-action supply-chain attack.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
